titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the manuscript "Dependencies of Mauritius" from H. A'C. Bergne AD.MC.162
titlestmt.author: Brad Bauer

publicationstmt.publisher: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

address.addressline: New York University Abu Dhabi, C-2

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-02-23 22:42:21 +0400.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: English, Latin script.

repository.corpname: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

did.unittitle: Dependencies of Mauritius [manuscript], by H. A'C. Bergne, Seychelles, A.D. 1900

physdesc.extent: containing one manuscript, 144 pages, of which 64 pages are marked.

did.abstract: Manuscript text, by Hervey A'Court Bergne, a British merchant based in the Seychelles, describing most of the smaller western Indian Ocean islands, which are today variously part of the Seychelles, Mauritius, and the Maldives; containing information on the islands' geography, economies, and people. Illustrated with 11 hand-drawn maps.
did.physdesc: Tall, narrow, quarto manuscript, 32 x 15 cm., original card bindin with modern marbled card cover. Ownership marks on flyleaft of "H. A'C. Bergne, Seychelles, A.D. 1900" written in pen, and just below that, H. A'C. Bergne, Lee Common, Bucks".

accessrestrict.head: Conditions governing access
accessrestrict.p: The collection is open for use in the Reading Room of the Archives and Special Collections department of the NYU Abu Dhabi Library.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The manuscript consists of 144 numbered pages, a little over half of which are blank. A numbered list of all of the islands included in this survey precedes the two parts of this manuscript. The first section (pages 3-30) describes the islands numbered 1-15, chiefly ones that are included in the present-day nation of Mauritius. The second part (pages 32-124) focuses on the Seychelles Archipelago, and covers the islands numbered from 16-24. Two additional pages at the end describe the Maldive Islands.

bioghist.head: Biographical note
bioghist.p: Hervey A'Court Bergne (1871-1941) was born on 26 April 1871 in West Brompton, in the borough of Kensington and Chelsea, London, the son of Frederick A'Court and Frances Anne (née Downes) Bergne. He was the eldest of five children and was raised in Kensington, where he married Eleanor Frances Eleson (born 1875) in 1898. Shortly after that, Bergne relocated to Mahé island, in the Seychelles, where along with Harold Baty, he founded the shipping and naval firm of Baty, Bergne and Company, the predecessor company to Union Lighterage. In addition, the firm was active in managing leases to some of the outer islands. It is unclear when Bergne returned to England, but records show that one of his sons, Denis, was born in Seychelles in 1907, while his other sons John (1902-1978) and Villiers (Bill) A'Court (1910-1989) were born in England. Census records show him and his family living in Wavendon Walton, Buckinghamshire the following year, and listing his occupation as a retired shipping agent, and formerly Consul for Italy in Mahé. During World War I he was listed in records as a temporary lieutenant in the Royal Naval Reserve, and following the war he lived variously in London and Buckinghamshire. He died at the age of 70 at Budleigh Salterton, Devon, on 28 October 1941.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: This manuscript belonged to the historian and philatelist of the Seychelles, Sue Hopson, and was sold with a number of other items from her library at auction by Dominic Winter Auctions, Cirencester, England in May 2025. The manuscript subsequently was resold in the antiquarian trade, and acquired by the NYU Abu Dhabi Library in January 2026.

scopecontent.head: Scope and contents
scopecontent.p: The existing manuscript was created by Hervey A'Court Bergne, beginning in 1900, as a project to document the islands of what was then the British colony of Mauritius, which encompassed the present-day nations of Mauritius and Seychelles, as well as the Chagos Archipelago. Bergne, whose chief occupation at the time was a merchant based on the island of Mahé, Seychelles, compiled several manuscript works about the islands.

The present work appears to have been begun in 1900, and it includes materials copied from other sources, as well as accounts drawn from Bergne's own voyages to specific islands. Among the portions copied were the first 15 entries, covering many of the Mascarene Islands that were British-held territories, except for the main island of Mauritius itself, which is never mentioned, other than as a reference point for the distance of other islands from it. Also included in this section are the Chagos Archipelago, Rodrigues, and some of the islands that comprise the modern nation of Seychelles. These descriptions were copied from a report of the Stipendiary Magistrate of Mauritius, compiled in 1880, and held at the time when Bergne used it by S.B. Hobbs, the chief surveyor in Seychelles. It is unknown whether that report still survives in any other form. These entries tend to be brief, listing the geographic coordinates, any agriculture or other products shipped from the islands, and concessions granted.

The second section, with islands numbered by Bergne from 16 through 24, chiefly focuses on the Seychelles Archipelago. While the main island of Mahé gets only a very brief mention, other islands received a more detailed narrative treatment, usually based on first-hand accounts of visitors, either Bergne or others. The section on the Aldabra Group of islands in Seychelles is the most extensive part of the manuscript, drawing from a report by S.C.E. Baty, the government surveyor, which had been compiled in December 1895, with four pages describing a voyage to these islands made by Baty between October and December 1895, and the remaining 24 pages detailing a wide range of information about these islands (Aldabra, Astove, Assumption, Menai and Wizard, the latter two part of the Cosmoledo group), including descriptions of flora and fauna, agriculture, industry, such as the mining of guano, forms of human settlement, shipwrecks, and marine life. This description of the Aldabra group also includes 7 of the 11 hand-drawn maps created by Bergne that have been pasted into this work.

The descriptions based on Bergne's first-hand visits (conducted in October 1901) are those of the Alphonse Group (18) and Providence Island (19), while a trip by A. Naylor on behalf of Bergne's company, Baty and Bergne (BBL) in December 1901 provides a description of the Amirantes Archipelago. Aside from the descriptions of the dependencies of Mauritius, a brief (two-page) description of the Maldive Islands, chiefly those situated around the central island of Malé, complete the volume.

Based on the numbering of the pages, it appears that Bergne laid out the contents with an eye to leaving many pages blank, with the intention of adding descriptions to other islands either as he had the opportunity to visit them or as he gathered further information about them from other sources.

userestrict.head: Conditions governing use
userestrict.p: Users are free to use materials in the collection in any way that is permitted by the copyright and related rights legislation that applies to its use.

prefercite.head: Preferred citation
prefercite.p: "Dependencies of Mauritius" [manuscript, H.A'C. Bergne; MC.162; Archives and Special Collections, New York University Abu Dhabi Library.

relatedmaterial.head: Related materials
relatedmaterial.p: The Paul Bergne collection (MC.077), containing materials related to the construction of dhows in the United Arab Emirates in the early 1970s, was created by Alexander Paul A'Court Bergne, the grandson of Hervey A'Court Bergne, while he was a British diplomat serving in Abu Dhabi from 1972 to 1974.

did.unittitle: List of dependencies, geographical coordinates, brief descriptions; as taken from data collected by Mr. Hobbs, of the Mauritius Survey Department
2. St. Brandon Group (otherwise Cargados or Caranzos Islands)
4/11. Chagos Archipelago (includes Diego Garcia and seven other islands)
14. St. Jean de Nove, or Farquhar Island
Narrative descriptions of islands, with maps
21.-24. Aldabra Group (with 6 maps), copied from report by S.C.E. Baty, Government Surveyor, Seychelles, including Aldabra, Assumption, Cosmoledo, and Astove Islands
Maldive Islands, with map [chiefly Malé and surrounding atolls]
